items.question: Who needs prior/posterior intuition in ML?
items.answer: ML engineers choosing regularization, data scientists reporting uncertainty, and researchers using Bayesian or variational models all need to know how priors shape posteriors after data arrive.

items.question: What is the posterior in one sentence?
items.answer: The posterior P(θ|x) is the updated probability distribution over parameters θ after observing data x, proportional to likelihood times prior.

items.question: When does the prior matter most?
items.answer: When data are scarce, noisy, or weakly informative relative to the parameter—early experiments, rare events, and high-variance groups show large prior effects.

items.question: Where does Ridge regression enter this story?
items.answer: Ridge is MAP estimation under a Gaussian prior on coefficients; the posterior mean shrinks toward the prior mean (often zero) while still fitting the data.

items.question: Why multiply likelihood and prior instead of adding?
items.answer: Multiplication in probability space corresponds to independence assumptions and ensures the result renormalizes to a proper posterior; in log space it becomes addition of log-likelihood and log-prior penalties.

items.question: How do you update a Beta–Bernoulli conjugate model?
items.answer: Start with Beta(α,β) prior on θ; after s successes in n trials, posterior is Beta(α+s, β+n−s)—add successes to α and failures to β.

items.question: How does a posterior differ from an MLE point estimate?
items.answer: MLE returns a single θ maximizing P(x|θ) ignoring P(θ); the posterior is a full distribution weighting plausible θ by both P(x|θ) and P(θ), so uncertainty and regularization are built in.

items.question: Weak prior vs strong prior—what changes in the posterior?
items.answer: A weak prior lets the likelihood dominate quickly; a strong prior keeps the posterior near the prior mean longer, reducing variance but risking bias if the prior is wrong.
